title: Chapter 33: The Power Goes Out
content: The lights died.

Not a flicker, not a warning — just gone, like a throat slit clean through. One second the Great Hall was blazing with chandelier light and the cacophony of two hundred people losing their minds, and the next there was nothing. Nothing but the howl of the blizzard outside, shaking the windows in their frames, and the sound of every voice in the room rising at once.
I couldn't see. I couldn't see anything.

My hands found the edge of the table. My pulse spiked — not fear, not yet, just the animal brain screaming *blind blind blind* in the dark. Someone near me screamed, high and sharp, and I heard glass shatter, a tray hitting marble, the chaotic percussion of people stumbling into each other, into furniture, into the walls.
Then the emergency lights kicked in.

Amber. Sick, sulfurous amber, turning the Great Hall into the inside of a ribcage, all shadow and honey-colored edges. Faces that had been polished an hour ago looked feral now — sweat-sheened, eyes too wide, mouths open. Someone was crying. Someone was yelling about cell service. Julian was somewhere near the far end of the hall, his voice cutting through the noise with practiced authority, but I couldn't see him through the press of bodies.
I could smell the fear. Sharp and sour, cutting through the remains of perfume and champagne and roasted meat. I could taste it on the back of my tongue — copper and adrenaline.
The blizzard hit the windows again, and the whole building groaned.
I turned toward the corridor — toward the exit, toward air, toward anywhere that wasn't this suffocating crush of panic — and that's when the hand closed around my arm.

Not a touch. Not a grab. A vice. Fingers digging in above my elbow, hard enough that I felt the bruise bloom instantly, a deep throb that shot all the way to my shoulder. I spun, and the amber light caught his face.
Robert.

But not Robert. Not the man I'd watched command boardrooms and dinner parties with the same effortless, smiling control. This was someone else entirely. This was the thing underneath. His hair had come loose from its careful styling, falling across his forehead in sweaty strands. His jaw was locked so tight the tendons in his neck stood out like cables. His eyes — those pale, assessing eyes that had measured me my entire life — were feral. Wild. The eyes of a man who'd just watched everything he'd built turn to ash and needed someone to burn for it.
He didn't say a word. He just dragged me.

My heels scraped against the marble. I tried to wrench my arm free, but his grip was iron and I was in heels and the crowd was too dense, too panicked, to notice — or maybe they noticed and looked away, because Robert Vance walking fast with his daughter's arm in his grip looked like authority, not violence. It looked like a father handling a situation. It looked like control.
And no one at Blackwood ever questioned control.

The corridor swallowed us. The amber emergency strips ran along the baseboards, turning the hallway into a gullet of shadow and dull, pulsing light. The noise of the Great Hall fell away behind us — muffled, distant, another world. All I could hear was my own breathing and the rasp of Robert's shoes on the stone.
He threw me against the wall.

My shoulder blades hit the paneling hard enough to rattle my teeth. The wainscoting bit into my spine through the silk of my dress. Before I could catch my breath, he was in my face — close enough that I could smell the whiskey on him, the salt stress-sweat beneath his cologne, the sourness of a man unraveling.
"You did this."

His voice was broken. Volcanic. Nothing like the measured, modulated tone he'd used my whole life — the voice that could make a death sentence sound like a courtesy. This was the voice underneath. The one that came from somewhere deep and feral and entirely beyond control.
"You little —" He stopped. Swallowed. His fingers flexed on my arm, and I felt the bruise deepen, spread, a constellation of pain. "You ruined EVERYTHING."
The word *everything* came out like he was tearing it from his own chest. Like it cost him blood just to say it.

I opened my mouth — to say what, I don't know. Deny it? Confirm it? Laugh in his face? But he was still talking, and the words were coming faster now, pouring out of him like he'd been holding them behind a dam for years and the dam had finally broken.

"I built this. I *built* this — from nothing, from the garbage, from a name that meant nothing and a bank account that was empty. And you — you strut in here with my name and my money and my education and you use it to—" He laughed. It was the worst sound I'd ever heard. Brittle and broken and completely insane. "You used it to destroy me."

I could feel the wall behind me, the carved grooves of the paneling pressing through the dress. I could feel the cold radiating from the stone — Blackwood's bones, ancient and indifferent, leaching the heat out of my body. I could feel the ache in my arm where his fingers were still dug in, five points of fire.
And I could feel something else. Something worse.
His hand moved.
Off my arm. Up. Slow, deliberate, like he was savoring it. His fingers found my throat.

Not choking. Not yet. Controlling. His palm flat against my skin, his thumb resting against the pulse point, feeling my heart slam against the inside of my chest like a caged thing. He could feel every beat. He *knew* I was afraid.

And I was. I was terrified. My body was screaming — run, fight, do something — but I was pinned between the wall and a man who had a foot and a hundred pounds on me, and the corridor was empty, and the lights were amber and the building was groaning and no one was coming.

"I made you," he said. Quiet now. The quiet was worse than the shouting. The quiet was ice. "I gave you everything. Every advantage. Every door opened. Every path cleared. You think you're clever? You think you're powerful? You are nothing but what I *allowed* you to be."
His thumb pressed. Not enough to cut off air. Enough to remind me he could.
"And you used it to destroy me."

I couldn't breathe. I could feel the pressure against my windpipe — controlled, measured, a man who knew exactly how much force to apply. I could smell the whiskey on his breath, sour and sharp. I could hear the blizzard outside, the wind screaming through the gap under the window at the end of the corridor, a sound like a woman keening.
But I could also hear something else. My own heartbeat. Loud and fast and *alive*.
"You're not my daughter," Robert said, and his voice was almost gentle, and that was the most terrifying thing of all. "You're a weapon I should have never loaded."

My vision was graying at the edges. Not from oxygen — from fury. From the absolute, crystalline clarity of what he was saying. *Weapon.* Not child. Not person. A thing he'd created and aimed and now regretted firing.
I couldn't speak. His hand was on my throat. But I could think. And what I thought, with perfect, burning clarity, was: *If I die here, at least I die having burned you first.*
I met his eyes. I didn't look away. I didn't cry. I didn't beg.
I would never beg.

Something shifted in his face — frustration, maybe, that I wouldn't break. That even with his hand on my throat, even in the dark, even with nothing, I wouldn't give him what he wanted. His grip tightened—
And then it was gone.

The pressure vanished from my throat so fast I gagged, drawing in air in a ragged, desperate gasp. Robert made a sound — a grunt, something between surprise and pain — and I blinked the gray from my vision and saw:
Heath.

He hadn't walked. He'd *moved*. One second he wasn't there and the next he was, and his hand was around Robert's wrist with a grip that made the bone creak. I could hear it — the subtle, terrible sound of forced compliance, of joint and tendon pushed past comfort into the territory before damage.
"Take your hands off her."

Not a request. Not a threat. A statement of physical law. The kind of voice that didn't need volume because it operated at a frequency below argument, below negotiation, below anything except brute, inescapable fact. Water is wet. Fire burns. Take your hands off her.

Robert's fingers opened. Reflex, not choice — the hand coming off my throat like it had been burned, his arm wrenched to the side by Heath's grip on his wrist. Heath released him and stepped forward, putting himself between Robert and me, and the corridor suddenly felt very small.

I could see the tension in Heath's back. Every muscle was coiled. His shoulders were locked, his spine rigid, his hands — his hands were fists at his sides, knuckles white, tendons standing out like they were trying to tear through the skin. He was trembling. Not from cold. Not from fear. From the effort of not turning around and putting Robert through the wall.

I could smell him — the cold sweat, the adrenaline, the faint cedar of his cologne almost burned away by the heat coming off his skin. His breathing was controlled, measured, each exhale deliberate, like he was counting.
He was counting. I realized that later. Counting breaths. Counting reasons not to kill.
"Don't." Robert's voice was thin. Shaking. The voice of a man who'd just realized he wasn't the most dangerous person in the room. "Don't pretend you have any right—"
"I'm not pretending anything." Heath still hadn't raised his voice. Still quiet. The quiet was a blade. "I'm telling you how this is going to go."
He turned.
Not all the way. Just enough. Just enough that Robert could see his face in the amber emergency light, and I could see it too, and what I saw made my breath catch.

Heath Cruz was furious. Not hot, explosive furious — that I'd seen before. This was different. This was the cold, controlled fury of a man who had decided exactly what he was going to do and was holding himself back from it by the thinnest possible margin. His jaw was set. His eyes were black in the amber light, and they held Robert's with the steady, patient focus of a predator.
"If you touch her again," he said, "I will end you."
Silence. The wind howled. The building shuddered.
"Not the board." Each word deliberate. Placed like a stone in a wall. "Not the school. Not your reputation, not your money, not your name. You."
He stepped closer. Robert stepped back. The corridor was narrow and the light was amber and Heath filled every inch of it.
"Personally. And I'll make it last."

The restraint. That was the thing. He wanted to hit Robert — I could feel it radiating off him, the need to strike, the coiled violence of a man defending something he couldn't name. Every line of his body was a weapon drawn and sheathed, drawn and sheathed, the restraint costing him more than the act ever would.

And Robert — Robert, who had never been afraid of anything in his life, who had stared down competitors and regulators and his own family with the same unblinking authority — Robert stepped back again. And again. And again.

He retreated down the corridor. Away from us. Into the dark between the emergency lights, where the amber faded to shadow and the shadow faded to nothing. I watched his face as he went — the disbelief, the fear, the recalculating. A man who'd just learned, for the first time, what it felt like to be the prey.
He turned a corner. He was gone.
But not far enough. Never far enough.

I stayed against the wall. My legs were shaking — I hadn't noticed when it started, but now I could feel it, the fine tremor in my thighs, the way my knees wanted to buckle. My throat throbbed where his hand had been. I could feel it in pulse-beats, the hot, tender ache of compressed tissue, the beginning of a bruise that would darken by morning. My arm was worse — I looked down and saw the red marks already rising, five finger-shaped welts that would be purple before the night was over.
Heath turned to me.

His hands were still shaking. I could see them — the tremor running through his fingers, the aftermath of what holding back had cost him. His face was different now. The cold fury was still there, banked like coals, but underneath it was something else. Something I'd never seen on him before.
Fear.
He was afraid. For me.
"Cat." My name in his mouth was barely a sound. He stepped closer, and his hand came up — slowly, carefully, giving me time to flinch or turn away — and his fingers touched my throat.
Gentle. So gentle it hurt worse than the bruising. His fingertips traced the place where Robert's hand had been, barely touching, reading the damage like braille. I felt his breath catch.
"Does it hurt to swallow?"
I shook my head. It did hurt, a little, but I wasn't going to say that.

His hand moved. Down my arm. Finding the bruise — the five marks, the red rising to purple — and his fingers stopped there, pressing against the edge of the discoloration with a touch so light it was almost not there. I could feel the warmth of his palm. The calluses on his fingertips. The residual tremor.
"Cat." My name again. His eyes were on the bruise, then on my face, searching — not for damage, I realized. For permission. For some signal that I was still here, still me, still intact.
I nodded.
I don't know what I was affirming. That I was okay? That I could stand? That I wanted him to keep touching me? Yes. All of it.
He pulled me in.

Not romantic. Not strategic. Not any of the things that had defined the strange, combative intimacy between us since the beginning. He just pulled me against his chest, his arms around my back, his chin against the top of my head, and I heard him exhale — a long, shaking breath, like a man who'd been holding it underwater.

My hands found the back of his shirt. I gripped it — fisted the fabric, knuckles pressing into the muscles of his back, holding on like he was the last solid thing in a building that was coming down around us. The cotton was damp with sweat. I could feel his heartbeat against my cheek, fast and hard, not yet calmed. I could smell the cedar and the adrenaline and the cold air still clinging to his skin from wherever he'd been before he saw me, before he moved, before he crossed the Great Hall in a heartbeat because Robert had his hands on my throat.

I didn't cry. I don't cry. Not anymore. But I held on, and the not-crying was its own kind of violence — the force of keeping it in, of refusing to break even now, even here, even with his arms around me and his heartbeat in my ear and my father's fingerprints darkening on my skin.
The wind slammed into the building. Somewhere, glass cracked. The emergency lights pulsed, amber and shadow, amber and shadow.
"He won't come near you again," Heath said into my hair. Not a promise. The same kind of statement as before. Physical law.
I believed him.

But I also knew — with the same cold clarity that had kept me meeting Robert's eyes with his hand on my throat — that Robert was still out there. In the dark, in the storm, in the bones of this building. A man with nothing left to lose was the most dangerous thing in the world, and we were trapped in the same fortress with him while the blizzard raged outside and the power lay dead in the snow.
I held on tighter.
The fire was burning. The blizzard was howling. And in the dark corridor, we were finally holding each other.
But Robert Vance was loose in the dark — and a man with nothing left to lose has nothing left to fear.
